facts.model:
CURRENT SECTION-4 MODEL (修論2025_Takumi/04-theoretical.tex, read in full).

Definition 1 (Field-mismatch tax), 04-theoretical.tex:24-30, verbatim: "Let $\mathsf{op}$ be a field operation over $\mathbb{F}_p$ used inside a verification predicate $\Pred$, and let $\mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\mathsf{op})$ denote the number of zkVM guest cycles required to emulate $\mathsf{op}$ by software ... The \emph{field-mismatch tax} of $\mathsf{op}$ is \[ \mathsf{FMT}(\mathsf{op}) = \frac{\mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\mathsf{op})}{\mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{nat}}(\mathsf{op})}. \]" with cumulative emulated cost (lines 31-37): "$\mathsf{Cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\Pred) = \sum_{\mathsf{op}\in\mathsf{ops}(\Pred)} \mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\mathsf{op})$".

Precompile accounting, 04-theoretical.tex:40-48, verbatim: "$\mathsf{Cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}^{\mathsf{pre}}(\Pred) = \sum_{\mathsf{op}\in\mathsf{ops}(\Pred)\setminus\mathsf{Pre}} \mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\mathsf{op}) + |\{\mathsf{op}\in\mathsf{ops}(\Pred)\cap\mathsf{Pre}\}| \cdot \mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{syscall}}$. The performance benefit of adding a primitive to the precompile set is, by this accounting, the difference $\mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{em}}(\mathsf{op}) - \mathsf{cyc}_{\mathsf{syscall}}$ summed over its occurrences."

TERMS THE MODEL HAS: only guest-cycle terms (cyc_em, cyc_nat, cyc_syscall, their sums). TERMS IT LACKS: any prover-side term — no trace-area term (rows x columns of the precompile AIR), no per-shard commitment cost, no core-chip-width term. The lack is ACKNOWLEDGED IN PROSE but not modeled, 04-theoretical.tex:48 verbatim: "This is a \emph{guest-trace} reduction: it counts cycles removed from the emulated execution, not the prover's wall-clock cost. The precompile shifts those cycles into a relation-specific AIR whose proving cost is charged on the host side, so a positive guest-cycle benefit need not translate into a faster prover, and Section~\ref{sec:evaluation} reports a primitive (Griffin) for which it does not." So the caveat sentence exists, but the formal model itself prices benefit purely in cycles removed, with no quantitative term for what the AIR adds. Also relevant: 04-theoretical.tex:21 cites PLUM §4.2's 105,952/116,285 R1CS constraints (≈91%) as hash share, explicitly flagged as "not a measured zkVM-cycle share".

CRUCIAL CORROBORATION FROM SP1's OWN CODE — SP1 internally prices prover cost by TRACE AREA, not cycles: (a) opts.rs:11-12 "The trace area threshold for a shard. pub const ELEMENT_THRESHOLD: u64 = (1 << 28) + (1 << 27)" — shards are split when accumulated trace elements (rows x width summed over chips), not cycles, exceed the threshold; (b) utils.rs:117-154 `cost_and_height_per_syscall`: per-syscall prover cost = rows_per_event * costs[air_id] + costs[control_air_id] + memory/global terms, where costs[] comes from artifacts/rv64im_costs.json whose entries empirically equal chip trace WIDTHS (verified: KeccakPermute=2640=measured width, Add=33=measured, Uint256MulMod=371=measured, Uint256MulModUser=419=measured); (c) gas.rs:97-98 "0.3 * trace_area + 0.1 * complexity ≈ (3 * trace_area + complexity) / 10; let gas = (3 * trace_area + complexity) / 10" — SP1's gas model weights trace area 3:1 over instruction complexity. The repaired model's shape (prove-time ≈ f(Σ_chips rows x width per shard)) is therefore SP1's own internal accounting, which the thesis's §4 model does not contain.
facts.air_term:
GRIFFIN CHIP DIMENSIONS (measured by compiling a temporary width-probe test against the fork at submodules/sp1, run via `cargo test -p sp1-core-machine --test width_probe`; probe deleted after use; widths are size_of::<Cols<u8,M>>() exactly as BaseAir::width() computes them, air.rs:100-102,449-453):

- GriffinFp192Chip (per-round chip, crates/core/machine/src/syscall/precompiles/griffin_fp192/air.rs): trace WIDTH = 3,819 columns (both SupervisorMode and UserMode). Cross-check by hand-count of GriffinFp192Cols struct (air.rs:128-426): 26 FieldOpCols x 127 cells (FieldOpCols = result 32 + carry 32 + witness 63 limbs; field_op.rs:32-37, fp192.rs:101-104 Limbs=U32/Witness=U63, asserted fp192.rs:138-139) + 7 FieldLtCols x 34 cells (range.rs:21-28: byte_flags 32 + 2) + 2 state arrays x 128 + 23 scalar/flag cells = 3302+238+256+23 = 3,819. EXACT match.
- ROWS per permutation = 14 (one row per round): NB_ROUNDS=14 (air.rs:98); num_rows = nb_events * NB_ROUNDS (air.rs:1158-1159), padded to next multiple of 32 (air.rs:1161); executor mirror air.rs(executor):495 "Self::GriffinFp192 => 14".
- GriffinFp192ControlChip (controller.rs:96-133): width = 265 (supervisor) / 289 (user) columns, 1 row per syscall (controller.rs:137 "One row per GRIFFIN_FP192_PERMUTE syscall").
- Per-permutation trace area (derived): 14 x 3,819 + 265 = 53,731 cells (supervisor). PLUM-80's 1,052 permutations: 1,052 x 53,466 = 56,246,232 round-chip cells + 1,052 x 265 = 278,780 controller cells ≈ 56.5M trace cells before padding (cell3 doc's "~37M" estimate at sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:85-90 used ~96 cells/FieldOpCols and ~33 ops; actual is 127 cells and 26 ops + state/range cells).

COMPARISON CHIP WIDTHS (same probe + rv64im_costs.json): KeccakMemCols = 2,640 columns at 24 rows/permutation (executor air.rs rows_per_event: KeccakPermute => 24; columns.rs:21) — i.e. Griffin row is 1.45x wider than keccak's and SP1's own vendor keccak precompile ALSO carries a large area bill (24 x 2,640 = 63,360 cells/permutation, comparable to Griffin's 53,466). ShaCompress = 80 rows/event at cost 206, ShaExtend = 48 rows at 128 (rv64im_costs.json + executor air.rs rows_per_event). Uint256MulCols = 371/419 wide, 1 row/syscall. Core ALU chips are NARROW: Add = 33, Sub = 33, Mul = 82 columns (probe + rv64im_costs.json) — this is the "per-cycle core area" side of the trade: one emulated-Griffin cycle adds ~33-82-column rows to narrow chips, while one precompiled permutation adds 14 rows x 3,819 columns to the fat chip.

STALE-COST FINDING: the executor's shard-splitting/gas table artifacts/rv64im_costs.json lists GriffinFp192 = 348 and GriffinFp192Control = 682, but the real widths are 3,819 and 265. Since every other checked entry equals the true width, SP1's sharding model under-budgets a Griffin syscall at 14x348+682 = 5,554 elements vs real 14x3,819+265 = 53,731 — a 9.7x underestimate. Shards containing Griffin events therefore carry ~10x more real trace area than ELEMENT_THRESHOLD intends, which plausibly also worsened Cell 2's memory pressure.
facts.postdiction: WHAT THE CURRENT §4 MODEL PREDICTS FOR CELL 2 vs CELL 3 (and why it is wrong). The model's only cost functional is Cyc_em (04-theoretical.tex:31-37,40-48): prover effort is monotone in guest cycles. Measured guest cycles: Cell 2 = 125,504,123, Cell 3 = 110,795,424 (four_scheme_benchmark.md:61-62) — Cell 2 is 1.13x HIGHER in cycles (four_scheme_benchmark.md:93). So a strictly cycle-proportional reading predicts prove(Cell2) ≈ 1.13x prove(Cell3); the looser reading the thesis narrative used (precompile removes the hash workload entirely from the guest, so the Griffin arm should at worst match the software-hash control) predicts prove(Cell2) ≲ prove(Cell3). Measured: prove(Cell2) = 1,952,069 ms = 32.53 min (sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:26) vs prove(Cell3) = 797,009 ms = 13.28 min (sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:50) — ratio 2.449 (cell3 md:73 "2.45x (Cell 2 is slower)"). The cycle-only model misses a factor of 2.45/1.13 ≈ 2.2 that is attributable to the area Cell 2's chips add per cycle: Cell 2 commits ~56.5M Griffin-chip cells (14 rows x 3,819 cols x 1,052 permutations + controller) that exist in NO term of the model, while Cell 3's SHA-3 cycles land in 33-82-column core chips. Against Cell 1 the model's sign is right: 56.4x cycle reduction (7,082,608,888 -> 125,504,123, four_scheme_benchmark.md:64,61,91) and Cell 1 indeed DNF (OOM 1m45s / non-term 3h6m, four_scheme_benchmark.md:73) — consistent with requirement (b): the cycle reduction is real and feasibility-restoring; what the model lacks is the ADDED term (AIR rows x AIR width) that makes Cell 2 slower than Cell 3 despite it.

derivations.model:
REPAIRED COST MODEL (replaces the cycles-only accounting of 04-theoretical.tex:24-48).

STARK prover work per shard is quasi-linear in committed trace area, summed over chips: trace generation, LDE/commitment (Merkle), and opening each touch every committed cell. SP1's own internals price cost this way: shards are split on a trace-area threshold, ELEMENT_THRESHOLD = (1<<28)+(1<<27), commented "The trace area threshold for a shard" (crates/core/executor/src/opts.rs:11-12); per-syscall prover cost is computed as rows_per_event*costs[air_id] + costs[control_air_id] + touched_addresses*costs[MemoryLocal] + 2*touched*costs[Global] + costs[SyscallPrecompile] + costs[Global] (crates/core/executor/src/utils.rs:117-143), where costs[] entries equal chip trace widths (rv64im_costs.json: Add=33, Mul=82, Uint256MulMod=371, KeccakPermute=2640); and gas = (3*trace_area + complexity)/10 (crates/core/executor/src/vm/gas.rs:97-98), i.e. SP1 weights area 3:1 over instruction complexity.

THE MODEL. Prove time
  T ≈ T_0 + κ_core · A_core + Σ_p κ_p · A_p,  with
  A_core = ā_core · C            (C = guest cycles; ā_core = average committed cells per cycle across core chips: instruction chip width 33-82 plus memory/range/byte bookkeeping; not measured for these runs, symbolic, lower-bounded by 33 = the Add-chip width SP1 itself charges per add)
  A_p    = E_p · a_p,   a_p = r_p · w_p + w_ctrl,p + m_p   (E_p = precompile events; r_p = rows per event; w_p = main-trace width of the precompile chip; w_ctrl,p = controller-chip width, 1 row/event; m_p = memory/global bookkeeping per event from utils.rs:138-143)
  T_0 = fixed prove overhead (Griffin smoke prove with 1 syscall = 13.04 s, sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:113-114, sets its scale).
The κ's are per-chip cost-per-cell constants absorbing LDE blowup, Merkle hashing, constraint-evaluation (quotient) work, and logup auxiliary columns; equal-κ is the first-order model, and the data force κ_pre > κ_core (see postdiction).

WHAT THE OLD MODEL GETS WRONG: Cyc_em^pre (04-theoretical.tex:40-48) prices a precompile's benefit as cyc_em(op) - cyc_syscall summed over occurrences, i.e. only the A_core reduction. It has no A_p term, so it assigns the Griffin AIR's 56.5M committed cells (derivation in air_term) a cost of zero. The prose caveat at 04-theoretical.tex:48 acknowledges the host-side charge but the formal model never prices it.

SUBSTRATE-SELECTION INEQUALITY (goal d). A precompile for operation op pays in prove wall-clock iff, per event,
  δ_cyc(op) · ā_core · κ_core  >  (r_p · w_p + w_ctrl,p + m_p) · κ_pre
(cycles removed per event, times core area per cycle, exceeds the AIR area added per event, weighted by the per-cell cost ratio). Griffin vs Cell 1: δ_cyc ≈ (7,082,608,888 - 125,504,123)/1052 = 6,613,217 cycles/permutation removed vs 53,731 cells/permutation added, so the precompile pays iff κ_pre/κ_core < 123·ā_core; at ā_core = 33 that bound is 4,062, far above even the fitted κ_pre/κ_core ≈ 250, hence Cell 1 DNF → Cell 2 32.5 min is exactly what the model predicts and the 56.4x cycle cut (four_scheme_benchmark.md:91) remains real and feasibility-restoring (goal b). Cell 2 vs Cell 3 is a different comparison (hash-family choice, both arms feasible): there the Griffin arm pays BOTH a positive cycle delta AND the full AIR area, so the model predicts Cell 2 slower unconditionally (goal a, see postdiction).

VENDOR-PRECOMPILE COROLLARY (goal c). The vendor keccak precompile's per-event area is 24 rows × 2,640 cols + 634 controller = 63,994 cells (executor air.rs:491 rows_per_event KeccakPermute => 24; rv64im_costs.json KeccakPermute=2640, KeccakPermuteControl=634), LARGER than Griffin's 53,731. So any precompile, vendor or bespoke, converts core area into AIR area at the same structural exchange rate, and wall-clock speedup = (ā·C_before + A_before)/(ā·C_after + E·a_p + A_after) + T_0 effects, which is far below the cycle ratio C_before/C_after whenever E·a_p is comparable to ā·C_after. This is why cycle counts collapse (17-39x in the caller-provided Anoma k256 benchmark, not verified against any repo file) while prove wall-clock improves modestly: the model says cycle ratio is an upper bound on prove-time ratio that is approached only when the precompile's per-event area is negligible against the remaining core area.
derivations.air_term:
EXPLICIT AIR-AREA TERM, Griffin Fp192, all dimensions read from source this session.

Per-row width w_G = 3,819 cells, exact. BaseAir::width() = size_of::<GriffinFp192Cols<u8,M>>() (air.rs:100-102), and the repr(C)/AlignedBorrow struct (air.rs:128-426) hand-counts to: 26 FieldOpCols (sbox1_sq, sbox1_cube, mds_out[4], rc_add[4], l_sq_2, quad_mul_2, l_sq_3, quad_mul_3, sbox0_sq, sbox0_cube, alpha_l_2, alpha_l_3, temp_2, temp_3a, temp_3b, temp_3, sum_2, quad_factor_2, sum_3, quad_factor_3) × 127 cells each (result 32 + carry 32 + witness 63; field_op.rs:32-37; fp192.rs NumLimbs: Limbs=U32, Witness=U63, with tests asserting NB_LIMBS=32, NB_WITNESS_LIMBS=63) + 7 FieldLtCols (rc_add_range_check[4], sbox1_cube_range_check, quad_mul_2_range_check, quad_mul_3_range_check) × 34 cells (byte_flags 32 + 2 comparison bytes; range.rs:21-28) + 2 state arrays × 4 lanes × 32 limbs = 256 + 23 scalar cells (is_real, is_first_round, is_last_round, round_idx, is_round_r[14], clk_high, clk_low, ptr_addr[3]) = 3,302 + 238 + 256 + 23 = 3,819.

Rows per permutation r_G = 14: NB_ROUNDS = 14 (air.rs:98), num_rows = nb_events × NB_ROUNDS (air.rs:1158-1159), executor mirror crates/core/executor/src/air.rs:495 (GriffinFp192 => 14). Padding is only to the next multiple of 32, min 16 (air.rs:1161 via next_multiple_of_32; sp1-hypercube util.rs:50-58), and fixed power-of-two shapes are OFF in this fork (record.rs:502 fixed_log2_rows returns None; preprocessed_shape is set to None at program.rs:78,124 and assigned nowhere in the prover), so padding adds at most 31 rows × 3,819 ≈ 118K cells per shard, negligible.

Controller chip: 1 row per GRIFFIN_FP192_PERMUTE syscall (controller.rs:135-139), STATE_NUM_WORDS = 16 (controller.rs:76); width w_ctrl = 265 cells (supervisor) per the gather pass's compiled size_of probe; struct shape re-read this session (controller.rs:96-133) is consistent, exact cell count of its sub-operations was not re-derived.

Per-event memory/global bookkeeping by SP1's own formula (utils.rs:138-143, supervisor): m_G = 16×costs[MemoryLocal=20] + 2×16×costs[Global=241] + costs[SyscallPrecompile=10] + costs[Global=241] = 320 + 7,712 + 10 + 241 = 8,283 cells/event.

PER-PERMUTATION AIR AREA: a_G = 14 × 3,819 + 265 = 53,731 cells (chip-only); 62,014 cells including bookkeeping.
PLUM-80 VERIFY (E_G = 1,052 permutations, four_scheme_benchmark.md:61): A_Griffin = 1,052 × 53,731 = 56,525,012 ≈ 56.5M committed cells chip-only (1,052 × 62,014 = 65,238,728 ≈ 65.2M with bookkeeping). Total Griffin rows 1,052 × 14 = 14,728: the chip is wide and short.

This corrects the stale in-repo estimate of "~37M trace cells" (sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:85-90, which used ~33 ops × ~96 cells) and resolves the open TODO at four_scheme_benchmark.md:136-143: the missing factor was the true per-op FieldOpCols size (127 cells) and the full 26-op + range-check + state column inventory.

STALE COST-TABLE FINDING: rv64im_costs.json budgets GriffinFp192 = 348 and GriffinFp192Control = 682, but the real widths are 3,819 and 265. Every other checked entry equals the true width (KeccakPermute 2640, Add 33, Uint256MulMod 371). SP1's shard splitter therefore budgets a Griffin syscall at 14×348 + 682 = 5,554 elements vs real 53,731, a 9.7x underestimate, so Griffin-bearing shards carry up to ~10x more real area than ELEMENT_THRESHOLD intends, a plausible contributor to Cell 2's memory pressure (81.31% peak, sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:28). Note the total Griffin area (56.5M cells) is below one tuned shard budget (2^26 = 67M), so this mis-budgeting distorts shard packing locally rather than globally.

CORE-SIDE COMPARISON UNITS: one emulated cycle lands in narrow chips (Add/Sub = 33, Mul = 82 columns, rv64im_costs.json) at 1 row/cycle plus bookkeeping; one precompiled permutation lands 14 rows in a 3,819-column chip. The UINT256_MUL precompile both arms share: 69,396 × 371 = 25,745,916 cells (Cell 2) vs 69,385 × 371 = 25,741,835 (Cell 3), difference 4,081 cells, which cancels in the comparison.
derivations.postdiction:
POSTDICTION OF THE CELL 2 vs CELL 3 INVERSION.

Measured: prove(Cell 2) = 1,952,069 ms = 32.53 min (sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:26), prove(Cell 3) = 797,009 ms = 13.28 min (sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:50), ratio 2.449 (sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:73), under identical tuning (SHARD_SIZE 2^22, ELEMENT_THRESHOLD 2^26, HEIGHT_THRESHOLD 2^20, TRACE_CHUNK_SLOTS 2, RAYON 8; cell2 md:41-45, cell3 md:34-42). Cycles: C2 = 125,504,123, C3 = 110,795,424 (four_scheme_benchmark.md:61-62), ratio 1.13 (line 93).

(a) SIGN. In the repaired model, T2 - T3 ∝ κ_core·ā_core·(C2 - C3) + κ_G·A_Griffin + κ_u·ΔA_uint256 = κ_core·ā_core·14,708,699 + κ_G·56,525,012 + κ_u·4,081. Every term is non-negative for every parameter assignment (ā_core ≥ 0, κ ≥ 0), because Cell 2 has BOTH more cycles AND the entire Griffin AIR area, while the uint256 areas cancel to 4,081 cells. So the repaired model postdicts Cell 2 slower unconditionally, from the chips' actual dimensions alone. The old model's narrative reading (the precompile arm should at worst match the software-hash control, since the hash left the guest) is excluded: it corresponds to dropping the A_Griffin term, i.e. pricing 56.5M committed cells at zero.

(b) MAGNITUDE, honestly bounded. Equal-κ ratio: R(ā) = (125.504·ā + 56.525 + 25.746) / (110.795·ā + 25.742) in millions of cells. R(0) = 82.271/25.742 = 3.196; R(∞) = 1.133. The measured 2.449 lies inside the band [1.133, 3.196]: the dimensional model brackets the measurement. Exact match requires equal-weight ā* = 0.132 cells/cycle (from 19.229 = 145.834·ā), or 0.192 including the 8,283-cell/event bookkeeping term. But SP1's own accounting charges at least 33 cells per cycle (Add width), and at ā = 33 the equal-κ model gives R = 4,223.9/3,682.0 = 1.147, barely above the pure cycle ratio. So linear-in-area with uniform per-cell cost recovers the sign and roughly half of log(2.449/1.133) but leaves a residual factor of 2.449/1.147 ≈ 2.14. Fitting the two-weight model gives κ_G/κ_core = 7.584·ā_core, i.e. ≈ 250 at ā = 33: a committed Griffin-chip cell would have to cost ~250x a core cell. Mechanism candidates, none invented as constants: (i) constraint-evaluation and logup work per Griffin row vastly exceeds a 33-column ALU row's (26 polynomial mod-p identities of 63+ coefficients each plus ~26×127 ≈ 3.3K byte-range lookups per row, vs a handful per ALU row; SP1's own gas model carries a separate non-area complexity term, gas.rs:97-98); (ii) the chip is wide-short (3,819 cols × ~14,728 rows total, spread over ~30 shards at SHARD_SIZE 2^22, so a few hundred rows per shard), and per-shard per-chip fixed costs (commit/open every column) penalize wide-short matrices; (iii) the 9.7x stale cost-table under-budget lets the splitter pack Griffin shards past the tuned memory envelope (Cell 2 peaked 81.31% vs Cell 3's 80.92%). Deciding among these needs per-shard, per-chip prover profiling, which no read file contains.

(c) CONSISTENCY WITH THE 56.4x CYCLE CUT AND CELL 1. The same fitted weights postdict Cell 1's infeasibility: Cell 1 commits ā·7,082,608,888 ≥ 233.7B core cells (at ā = 33) vs Cell 2's ā·125.5M + 56.5M ≈ 4.2B; even at the fitted κ_G/κ_core = 250, the Griffin area's weighted cost (≈ 250 × 56.5M = 14.1B core-cell equivalents) is ~16x below Cell 1's core area, so the precompile pays massively against its own software baseline (and Cell 1 indeed DNF: OOM at 1m45s, non-termination at 3h6m, projection ~30h, four_scheme_benchmark.md:73). The 56.4x cycle reduction (four_scheme_benchmark.md:91) is real guest-side relief; the inversion arises only against the SHA-3 control, whose hash cost stays in 33-82-column core chips and never buys a 3,819-column commitment.

(d) The substrate-selection reading: PLUM's Griffin permutation removed ≈ 6.61M cycles each (huge δ_cyc, precompile pays vs its own baseline) but a SHA-3 software hash for the same protocol role costs so few cycles that the AIR area it would avoid is cheaper than the AIR area Griffin's precompile adds. A precompile pays only against the workload it replaces, and per event only when δ_cyc · ā_core · κ_core > a_p · κ_pre.

derivations.model:
REPAIRED COST MODEL (empirically fitted, two-term linear in trace area):

  T_prove ≈ k · C_guest + β · A_pre,   with  A_pre = N_perm · (R · W_round + W_ctrl)

where C_guest = guest cycles (the old FMT model's only variable, 04-theoretical.tex:24-48), and A_pre = trace cells the precompile AIR adds (rows × columns, summed over its chips). The first term is the surviving content of the §4 FMT model; the second term is what it lacked. Equivalently, in pure-area form T ≈ α·A_core + β·A_pre with A_core = a_core·C_guest + A_uint256 and k = α·a_core; a_core (core trace cells per guest cycle) was never measured, so the cycle form is the directly fittable one.

FITTED COEFFICIENTS (M5 Pro 24 GB, SP1 v6.2.1 fork, tuned env SHARD_SIZE=2^22 / ELEMENT_THRESHOLD=2^26 / 8 threads, identical on both arms — sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:41-45, sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:34-42):
  k = T3 / C3 = 797,009 ms / 110,795,424 cycles = 7.194e-3 ms/cycle (7.19 µs/cycle), fitted on Cell 3 which has zero Griffin rows (Griffin syscalls fired = 0, sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:53).
  β = (T2 − k·C2) / A_pre = (1,952,069 − 902,817) ms / 56,525,012 cells = 1,049,252 / 56,525,012 = 1.856e-2 ms/cell (18.56 µs per Griffin-chip cell) ≈ 997 ms per permutation ≈ 71 ms per 3,819-wide Griffin row.

DEGREES OF FREEDOM — UNDER-DETERMINATION STATED EXPLICITLY: two measured points (T2, T3), two coefficients (k, β) → zero residual dof; the fit reproduces both points by construction and is NOT yet a tested prediction. The fit is exact only under two closure assumptions: (i) k is arm-invariant (same core-work throughput in both runs; supported weakly by identical tuning and near-identical peak memory, 81.31% vs 80.92%, cell2:28/cell3:52, but not proven); (ii) the UINT256_MUL chip area is common-mode and cancels (69,396 vs 69,385 syscalls × 371 cols = 25,745,916 vs 25,741,835 cells, a 4,081-cell difference, negligible). A third closure is needed to split k into α·a_core: SP1's own shard budgets imply a_core ≤ 24 cells/cycle (default ELEMENT_THRESHOLD (2^28+2^27)/MAX_SHARD_SIZE 2^24 = 24; tuned 2^26/2^22 = 16; opts.rs:9-12) — budget ceilings, not measurements. Taking a_core ∈ [16, 24] gives α ∈ [0.30, 0.45] µs/cell and hence β/α ≈ 41–62: one Griffin-chip cell costs ~40–60× more prover time than one core-chip cell, or in the cleanest unit-free form, β/k = 2.58 — committing ONE Griffin trace cell costs the prover as much as ~2.6 removed guest cycles. β is an EFFECTIVE coefficient: it absorbs commitment, constraint evaluation (the chip carries 26 FieldOpCols per row, each a 32-limb modmul with 63 witness limbs and byte-lookups, air.rs:128-426 + field_op.rs:32-37), per-shard padding (≤31 rows × 3,819 × ~30 shards ≤ 3.6M cells ≤ 6.3% of A_pre, from next_multiple_of_32 at air.rs:1161), and any memory-pressure slowdown; these are NOT separable from two points.

WHY THE UNIFORM-COST VERSION FAILS (and what that teaches): if α = β (every trace cell priced equally), the predicted ratio T2/T3 = (a_core·C2 + U2 + A_pre)/(a_core·C3 + U3) = 1.15–1.16 for a_core ∈ [16,24] — right SIGN (Cell 2 slower, from both C2 > C3 and A_pre > 0) but a 2.1× magnitude shortfall vs the measured 2.449. The inversion's magnitude therefore requires the precompile-cell premium β >> α, which is the quantitatively new content.

VENDOR-PRECOMPILE CLAUSE (goal c): the model explains modest wall-clock wins from huge cycle cuts generically: per covered op, wall saving = k·cyc_em(op) − β_chip·A_chip(op). Vendor chips carry the same area bill — keccak: 24 rows × 2,640 cols = 63,360 cells/permutation (probe + columns.rs:21 per gather pass; KeccakPermuteControlUser=682 in rv64im_costs.json), comparable to Griffin's 53,731 — so the cycle ratio collapses while the wall ratio is throttled by β·A_chip. This is corroborated by SP1's OWN internal accounting, which prices proving by area not cycles: shards split on trace elements (opts.rs:11-12 'The trace area threshold for a shard'), per-syscall cost = rows_per_event × chip_cost + control + memory terms (utils.rs:128-143), and gas = (3·trace_area + complexity)/10 (gas.rs:97-98), with the cost table empirically equal to chip widths. The external Anoma k256 datapoint (17–39× cycle cut, modest prove gain) matches this shape but is caller-supplied and unverified in-repo.

SUBSTRATE-SELECTION INSIGHT (goal d): a precompile pays iff  k · (cycles removed) > β · (AIR rows added × AIR width), i.e. cycles_removed > 2.58 × cells_added on this stack. Both measured outcomes instantiate it: (i) Cell 1→Cell 2 (precompile on/off, same hash): k·Δcyc = 7.194e-3 × 6,957,104,765 = 50,047 s saved vs β·A_pre = 1,049 s added → pays by ~48×, the precompile is correctly a feasibility-recovery mechanism; (ii) Cell 3→Cell 2 (hash substitution): cycles removed = C3 − C2 = −14,708,699 (the Griffin arm spends MORE cycles) AND adds 56.5M cells → loses on both terms, postdicting the 2.45× inversion. The design rule: precompile the primitive whose software emulation is catastrophically cycle-heavy relative to its AIR area (192-bit modmul chains), not the primitive that is merely algebraically elegant — and when the predicate designer can choose the hash, a hash whose SOFTWARE cost on the substrate is cheap (SHA-3 on RISC-V) beats an algebraic hash plus a fat chip.
derivations.air_term:
THE AIR-AREA TERM, from the chip source (all verified by direct read this session):

A_pre = N_perm · (R · W_round + W_ctrl)  [+ per-shard padding ≤ 31 rows × W_round per shard]

- R = NB_ROUNDS = 14 rows per permutation: air.rs:98 (constant), air.rs:1158-1159 (num_rows = nb_events × NB_ROUNDS), air.rs:1161 (padded to next multiple of 32).
- W_round = 3,819 columns, verified by hand-count of GriffinFp192Cols (air.rs:128-426, width = size_of per air.rs:100-102): 26 FieldOpCols × 127 cells + 7 FieldLtCols × 34 cells + 2 state arrays × (4 lanes × 32 limbs) + 23 scalar cells = 3,302 + 238 + 256 + 23 = 3,819. Per-cell sizes verified: FieldOpCols = result(32) + carry(32) + witness(63) = 127 (field_op.rs:30-37; fp192.rs NB_LIMBS=32 / NB_WITNESS_LIMBS=63 asserted in test limb_count_matches_u256_field); FieldLtCols = byte_flags(32) + 2 comparison bytes = 34 (range.rs:19-28). The 26 FieldOpCols fields: sbox1_sq, sbox1_cube, mds_out[4], rc_add[4], l_sq_2, quad_mul_2, l_sq_3, quad_mul_3, sbox0_sq, sbox0_cube, alpha_l_2, alpha_l_3, temp_2, temp_3a, temp_3b, temp_3, sum_2, quad_factor_2, sum_3, quad_factor_3. The 7 FieldLtCols: rc_add_range_check[4], sbox1_cube_range_check, quad_mul_2_range_check, quad_mul_3_range_check. The 23 scalars: is_real, is_first_round, is_last_round, round_idx, is_round_r[14], clk_high, clk_low, ptr_addr[3].
- W_ctrl = 265 columns (supervisor), 1 row per syscall (controller.rs:137-139 'One row per GRIFFIN_FP192_PERMUTE syscall'); struct read at controller.rs:94-133 but its byte size taken from the gather-pass width probe, not recomputed here — it is 0.5% of A_pre, so the model is insensitive to it.

For Cell 2's measured N_perm = 1,052 (four_scheme_benchmark.md:61): per-permutation area = 14 × 3,819 + 265 = 53,731 cells; A_pre = 1,052 × 53,731 = 56,525,012 cells (14,728 event-rows). This RESOLVES the open TODO at four_scheme_benchmark.md:136-143: the old '37M' estimate used ~96 cells/FieldOpCols and ~33 ops; the correct figure is 56.5M from 127 cells × 26 ops + state/range/scalar cells.

STALE-COST FINDING (independent, actionable): rv64im_costs.json prices GriffinFp192 = 348 and GriffinFp192Control = 682 (read directly, lines 49-52 of the json), but the real widths are 3,819 / 265. Since shard splitting and the executor's syscall costing use this table (utils.rs:128-136), a Griffin syscall is budgeted at 14×348+682 = 5,554 elements vs real 53,731 — a 9.7× under-budget. Cell 2's shards therefore carried ~10× more real Griffin area than ELEMENT_THRESHOLD intended, a plausible contributor to both its memory pressure and a non-marginal share of the fitted β.
derivations.postdiction:
REQUIREMENT (a) — SIGN: the repaired model postdicts the inversion's sign structurally: T2 − T3 = k·(C2−C3) + β·A_pre is positive for ANY positive coefficients, because Cell 2 has both more cycles (125,504,123 vs 110,795,424, four_scheme_benchmark.md:61-62) and 56.5M Griffin cells that Cell 3 lacks (0 Griffin syscalls, sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:53). The old §4 model also has the cycle term but its narrative prediction ('precompile arm at worst matches the control') is falsified by magnitude: cycles alone predict 1.13×, uniform-area pricing predicts 1.15–1.16×, measured is 2.449× (1,952,069/797,009). The fitted decomposition of Cell 2's 32.53 min: 902,817 ms (46%) core work at Cell-3's throughput + 1,049,252 ms (54%) Griffin-chip area — i.e. over half of Cell 2's prove time is the term the FMT model does not contain. Per-shard cross-check: T3/≈27 shards ≈ 29.5 s/shard vs T2/≈30 shards ≈ 65 s/shard; the ≈35 s/shard excess for ≈491 Griffin rows/shard (1.88M cells) vs a ≈67M-cell core shard restates the β/α ≈ 40–60 premium.

REQUIREMENT (b) — the 56.4× cycle cut stays real: the k·C term retains it. Cell 1 at 7,082,608,888 cycles (four_scheme_benchmark.md:64) postdicts k·C1 = 50,949 s ≈ 14.2 h even before any uint256-chip area surcharge (Cell 1 fires 4,870,724 UINT256_MUL syscalls = 1.807e9 chip cells, whose effective rate is unconstrained by the Cell2/Cell3 pair since it is common-mode there). 14.2 h ≫ the 3 h bound and is DNF/OOM-consistent (measured: OOM 1m45s default, non-terminated 3h6m tuned, projected ~30 h, four_scheme_benchmark.md:73). The precompile cut a 14–30 h core-work bill to 15 min of core work plus a 17.5 min area bill — feasibility recovery, exactly as the Cell-3 doc's reframe states (sp1_plum_cell3_measurement.md:111-116).

REQUIREMENT (c) — vendor precompiles: modest wall wins from large cycle cuts follow from per-op accounting wall_save = k·cyc_em − β·A_chip with vendor chips carrying comparable area (keccak 63,360 cells/perm vs Griffin 53,731); SP1's own gas/shard accounting (gas.rs:97-98, opts.rs:11-12, utils.rs:128-143) already prices by area, corroborating the model's shape from the vendor's side.

REQUIREMENT (d) — selection rule with measured constants: precompile pays iff cycles_removed > (β/k)·cells_added = 2.58 × cells_added. Cell1→Cell2: 6.96e9 cycles removed vs 2.58×56.5e6 = 1.46e8 equivalent → pays ~48×. Cell3→Cell2: −14.7e6 cycles 'removed' plus 56.5e6 cells added → loses on both terms; postdicts the inversion.

CONSISTENCY ANCHOR: the single-syscall smoke prove = 13.04 s (sp1_plum_cell2_measurement.md:113-114) bounds marginal-per-permutation cost from above; the fitted 0.997 s/perm + ~12 s fixed overhead is consistent with it.

HONESTY CAVEAT: with 2 points and 2 coefficients the fit reproduces the data by construction; (a)'s sign and (b)'s Cell-1 feasibility are genuine out-of-fit postdictions, the 2.45× magnitude is in-fit. The model becomes testable with the one additional run named in open_gaps.

THE ONE OVER-DETERMINING MEASUREMENT: a Griffin-loop guest (extend the existing griffin smoke host) executing M GRIFFIN_FP192_PERMUTE syscalls with negligible other work, at M = 1,052 (matching Cell 2's permutation count, A_pre = 56.5M cells, ~zero core cycles). One ~20-min run discriminates the hypotheses and, combined with the existing 1-syscall smoke (13.04 s), gives a 2-point slope that separates fixed from marginal cost. Cheaper variant of the same idea: a second M (e.g. 2,104) pins linearity.

objections: The β/α ∈ [41,62] premium is one-sided and overstated: the a_core bracket [16,24] comes from ELEMENT_THRESHOLD/MAX_SHARD_SIZE ratios, which are CEILINGS on cells/cycle, not measurements. An upper bound on a_core yields only an upper bound β/α ≤ 62; nothing read establishes a_core ≥ 16, so a_core = 5–10 (plausible for ALU-dominated traces at 33–82-col chips) would give β/α ≈ 13–26. Worse, the observed shard counts are cycle-quantized (C2/2^22 = 29.92 ≈ 30, C3/2^22 = 26.4 ≈ 27), meaning ELEMENT_THRESHOLD apparently never bound — evidence that real a_core < 16, which pushes the premium DOWN. Only β/k = 2.58 cycles/cell is unit-free and bracket-independent; the 40–60× headline should be demoted to '≤ 62, lower end unconstrained'.

The 'common-mode UINT256_MUL cancels' framing hides a cycle-scaling mischarge: the uint256 chip area (25,745,916 vs 25,741,835 cells — the 4,081-cell count difference IS negligible) sits inside k = T3/C3, so k·C2 charges Cell 2 for 1.133× the uint256 cost although its area is flat. If uint256 cells price near β, that overcharge is ~63 s (recomputed: 477.9 s × 13.3%), i.e. up to ~6% of the 1,049 s residual, biasing β DOWN — the opposite direction of the stated 'β is an upper bound' claim. Both biases coexist; the derivation states only one.

The per-shard cross-check (29.5 vs 65.1 s/shard, restating β/α ≈ 40–60) is circular: it divides the same two totals by shard counts inferred from the same cycle figures, and additionally leans on the unconstrained [16,24] bracket and on the tuned-threshold 67M-cell 'core shard' figure, which is a budget knob, not a measurement. It corroborates nothing independent and should not be presented as a cross-check.

Quantization/blowup coverage is partial: only the multiple-of-32 padding is bounded (recomputed ≤ 6.28% of A_pre). Per-shard power-of-two height padding of the 3,819-wide chip (e.g. 491→512 rows/shard if spread evenly, 14,728→16,384 if clustered, +4–11%) and prove-time log-up/permutation columns multiplying effective width are named in open_gaps but unbounded; LDE blowup is uniform across chips only if the fork uses a single blowup factor, which was not verified. All of these inflate the effective β, so the selection rule 'cycles_removed > 2.58 × cells_added' is conservative against precompiles — a direction-of-error statement the derivation omits.

The smoke-test 'consistency anchor' is vacuous as stated: one point (13.04 s, 1 syscall) cannot separate fixed from marginal cost, so 'fitted 0.997 s/perm + ~12 s fixed is consistent' is satisfiable by almost any (fixed, marginal) split with marginal ≤ 13.04 s. It constrains nothing; the M=1,052 Griffin-loop run in open_gaps is the only real test and is correctly identified as load-bearing.

The sign postdiction (requirement a) is not distinctive: T2 > T3 follows from C2 > C3 under the OLD cycle-only model too (it predicts 1.13×, also positive). The repaired model's sole new content is the magnitude, which has zero residual dof — the derivation says this honestly, but downstream consumers should treat the model as a parametrization awaiting the Griffin-loop run, not a tested theory.

Cell-1 postdiction under-predicts the doc's own ~30 h projection by >2× (14.15 h recomputed); charging Cell 1's 1.807e9 uint256 cells at β adds 9.32 h → ~23.5 h, closing most of the gap. The derivation flags 'lower bound' but leaves the 2× discrepancy unremarked; the uint256-at-β reconciliation is a free consistency check worth stating.

Minor traceability: (i) supervisor widths (3,819/265/371) are used throughout but whether the Cells 1–3 runs executed the supervisor or user variants (3,819 same / 289 / 419 per costs.json GriffinFp192User=372, ControlUser=730 — read this session) was not verified; effect on A_pre ≤ 0.1%, but the choice is silent. (ii) keccak 24×2,640 and controller 265 are carried from a prior probe, fine per the fact sheet but correctly caveated. (iii) GriffinFp192Control=682 in costs.json exactly equals KeccakPermuteControlUser=682 — corroborates the placeholder/copy-paste hypothesis for the stale entries, worth adding to the stale-cost finding.

Arithmetic verdict for the record: every derived number reproduces (k = 7.1935e-3 ms/cyc; A_pre = 56,525,012; residual = 1,049,253 ms = 53.8% of T2; β = 18.563 µs/cell = 997.4 ms/perm = 70.9 ms/row; β/k = 2.580; uniform ratio 1.153–1.162 vs 2.449; stale factor 9.67×; payoff 47.7×; k·C1 = 14.15 h), and the repo spot-checks (prove_ms, cycles, syscall counts, costs.json, NB_ROUNDS, thresholds) all match. No dimension error, no UINT256 double-count (it appears only in k, never in A_pre, and is excluded from the Cell-1 term as an explicit lower bound), no untraceable number except the flagged Anoma datapoint.

prose.text:
We read this as a genuine
property of the pipeline rather than a measurement artefact, and the trace-area
accounting of Section~\ref{sec:theoretical}
(Equation~\eqref{eq:trace-area-model}) postdicts its sign from the chips'
committed dimensions. The Griffin chip emits $14$ rows of $3{,}819$ columns per
permutation (each row carries $26$ modular-arithmetic gadgets of $127$ cells
each, beside range checks and state limbs), so the $1{,}052$ permutations of one
verification commit $14\times 3{,}819\times 1{,}052\approx 5.6\times10^{7}$
cells, plus one controller row per permutation, that the SHA-3 arm never pays.
The shared \texttt{UINT256\_MUL} chip is common-mode ($69{,}396$ versus
$69{,}385$ events) and cancels, and the Griffin arm additionally executes $13\%$
more guest cycles ($1.26\times10^{8}$ versus $1.11\times10^{8}$, execute mode).
Both terms of Equation~\eqref{eq:trace-area-model} are therefore larger on the
Griffin arm, so the model places it behind the control for every non-negative
choice of the per-cell rates, granted a common per-cycle core area across the
two arms. Calibrating the constants on these two runs, the control arm prices
core work at $\kappa_{\mathsf{core}}\,\bar a_{\mathsf{core}}\approx
7.2\,\mu\mathrm{s}$ per guest cycle, and attributing the Griffin arm's residual
$17.5$~minutes to its $5.6\times10^{7}$ cells gives an effective
$\kappa_{\mathsf{Griffin}}\approx 19\,\mu\mathrm{s}$ per committed cell; in
unit-free form, committing one Griffin-chip cell costs the prover about as much
as removing $2.6$ guest cycles buys. Two measured points fit two constants, so
the magnitude is reproduced by construction; the out-of-fit content is the sign
and the orderings the same constants imply. Those orderings are consistent with
the rest of the table: the precompile-free baseline's $7.08\times10^{9}$ cycles
price at upwards of $14$~hours of core work alone (before the chip area of its
$4{,}870{,}724$ \texttt{UINT256\_MUL} events is charged), consistent with its
DNF, and against that baseline the precompile still pays by orders of magnitude,
removing about $6.6\times10^{6}$ cycles per permutation against
$5.3\times10^{4}$ cells added. The feasibility recovery and the inversion are
the same inequality (Equation~\eqref{eq:precompile-pays}) evaluated at two
operating points. What the precompile bought us was a finite measurement; it did
not buy a speedup, and the accounting says why: against a control whose hash
costs the substrate no chip at all, the Griffin arm pays the chip area and
recovers nothing.
